tax: Placed at family rank, Pelitic Earthkin is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a fine-grained sediment body, clay or silt, not coarse stone. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a fine-sediment body with its own wet/dry formation mode.
ap: The grouping is recognised by a slumping mud-mound or an upright dust-column. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a drying clay pan or a shed grey film — which survives even when the body disperses.
eco: Pelitic Earthkin occupies wet clay galleries and dry dust shafts. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a seep wets a clay bed or a static charge raises a dust bed and recur with seep refill or dust recharge, sustaining themselves on osmotic swelling or static earth-charge.
beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: they ambush from damp or drift through still air. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
traitsText: Shared characters: fine-sediment body, immunity to piercing, and medium-failure on drying or wetting. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: engulfing smother or abrasive choking. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
bg: In the Throat, Pelitic Earthkin matters because they make the clay galleries and dust shafts lethal in opposite ways. It lets survey reports sort smother hazards from abrasion hazards without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.
g: Pelitic Earthkin is the family-rank grouping of fine-sediment lithic elementals of clay and silt within the True Elementals. Its members share a body of fine sediment — wet clay or dry dust — rather than coarse rock, and divide below into genus groups by whether the sediment is wet and swelling or dry and abrasive. The Pelitic kin hunt by smother or abrasion, never by mass.
